sentences.text: Well, as I say, I probably don't recollect too much about him, because I was a child, and I-- I don't remember an awful lot.
I more or less told you what I remembered about him.
I-- I can't say that he did anything terrible to me personally.
I-- I don't think I have any lasting effects of any of the experiments he did.
I hope not, and I don't believe I do.
I mean, I don't recall him ever injecting anything into my body or my brother's body.
But I do know about him injecting other kids, and-- and I do know that he did terrible things to other children and other twins.
Basically, I'm lucky.
And my brother and I, I think, are basically lucky that nothing-- no permanent damage, certainly not physical permanent damage resulted from his experiments.
Well, I don't-- I don't-- I didn't have firsthand knowledge of it until actually a few years ago, when the twins had a meeting in Jerusalem.
We had a get together, and some of them described their-- their experiences.
And then that's when I really found out that some of them fared a lot worse than I did, because somehow, in those days, I don't-- I didn't even realize what may have happened to some of the others.
If a pair of twins disappeared suddenly, I probably would've noticed it.
But if I wasn't close to them, it probably wouldn't have made much difference in those days to me, because we were preoccupied with survival ourselves.
Well, we children didn't have to-- or twins didn't have to work, at least not until the very end.
We sort of had-- we could just sit around.
We-- we weren't expected to do any work.
We had, in fact, some, I recall, some games.
There was the occasional soccer game.
We weren't participating, but the older twins would participate in soccer games in that particular camp where we stayed.
And some of the SS would come and watch the games.
And there were games, you know, between two teams of inmates.
And-- So in that sense, we didn't have it as bad as a lot of other people.
I mean, we were preserved for experiments.
We weren't allowed to be touched.
In fact, at some point towards the end-- it must have been in the fall or late fall-- another doctor-- SS doctor-- by the name of Dr.
Thilo did select us out for-- for extermination.
And that was one of the low points of my life, I suppose.

We were-- there was a selection among the twins, and the ones, the children especially who were incapable of doing any work for future, I suppose future work, were selected for-- for the gas chambers.
And we knew-- we knew we were going to be selected, that we are being selected, because our numbers, two numbers were taken down.
And that was a clear sign that that night, we're going to be taken to the gas chambers.
And Mengele apparently didn't know about it.
It was another doctor who did it behind his back.
And there must have been some rivalry between them, or some such thing.
And when he was notified, he actually annulled it.
And we only found out about it later.
But it was Mengele that ordered us not to be taken away.
He did.
Oh, he saved my life in more ways than one, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even though he was a monster.
I'm sorry.
What other things that--
Yeah.
There were a number of incidents that, as a child, I'll never forget.
Things like when somebody would be caught in the electrified fence and get killed, and actually burn to death.
I mean, you can't forget those scenes.
You see them touching the wire, and some of them probably doing it because they wanted to commit suicide.
Other may-- others may have done it because they wanted to reach something near the fence, and they were actually drawn to the-- the current was like 10,000 volts, I think.
It was a pretty-- pretty powerful electrified fence.
And there were other things, where people were punished and beaten by the SS.
I mean, those are things you don't forget.
We had the-- the camp next to us was the gypsy camp.
There were, I think, something like 10 to 20,000 gypsies, mostly children and women.
And they were one night just taken away, all of them, to be gassed.
And you could hear their screaming.
That's the kind of thing you don't forget, and-- and things like that.
Now, towards the end, when the Russians were getting close to the camp, the Germans panicked.
And at that point, I don't even know if Mengele was still there or not.
But at that point, we were recruited to do some work.
What happened was the-- there was a camp on the other side.
One side was the gypsy camp, and the other side of us was a camp called Canada, of all things.
I don't know if you ever heard that name [LAUGHS] in connection with Auschwitz.
And Canada was the camp-- God only knows why they named it Canada-- but that was the camp where all the belongings were taken to by-- I mean the belongings of the deportees.
And they decided to have the whole thing burned, and they had huge fires.
And we were supposed to-- we were recruited to do some of that work.
And by that time it was winter.
It was cold, bitter cold.
And of course, we didn't have proper clothing.
And we-- and then we had to push some of these carts that had all the belongings and all the merchandise and everything on.
And I remember the wheels being frozen, and we had to-- it was just an incredible task, trying to move the stuff in that winter.
And of course, we were all weakened.
But even then, I mean, it just shows some of the things that you recall.
We were-- we were kids, and we had to push those carts, and it was very hard work.
And there was a young SS officer who was actually beating us.
And there were some other SS men who were under him.
And I recall one or two of them, when the officer didn't look, they would pass on some sugar cubes to us, you know.
And they were SS.
You know, but-- But it just shows that human nature, no matter what, I mean, you cannot generalize.
Now, maybe these same SS killed other Jews.
But at that point, they showed some humanity.
And when the officer didn't look, they would-- they would help us.
Either because they felt it was the end anyways, and they tried to-- maybe they tried to ingratiate themselves.
I don't know why, but there were these instances where-- where some humanity was demonstrated by some of these people.
And you don't forget these things.
But that was the time towards the end, when-- when we were-- we had a real tough time.
And as I say, the SS, they were panicking.
And at some point, just a few days prior to the-- our liberation, we were taken to-- they were all-- we were all being collected and taken to-- to Auschwitz proper from Birkenau.
And as I understand it, the plan was to have us all machine gunned somewhere in between, taken out of the camp.
And at some point, there were supposed to be some other SS waiting for us with machine guns.
But it turns out that they were gone.
The Russians were so close, they were just gone.
So we just marched into Auschwitz proper, the Auschwitz camp, from Birkenau.
And we stayed there a couple of days, or maybe a few days, until the Russians arrived.
Oh, we didn't know whether we were going to be liberated.
We-- we actually expected not to survive it.
I mean, the-- the common wisdom was that those who stay in the camp are going to be exterminated.
About a couple of weeks before, they organized transports, and the ones who felt they were-- they actually gave you the choice.
If you wanted to, you could-- you could march with a group of people and be-- you know, a forced march to-- towards Germany, towards-- away from the Russians.
And some of the-- some of the older twins actually decided to go with that transport.
Obviously, we weren't in any kind of condition, position, to undertake anything like that.
So we stayed.
But you know, they felt, and the common knowledge was-- or opinion was that those who stayed behind haven't got a chance.
As it turned out, a lot of those who did go didn't have a chance.
They were killed.
Some survived, some didn't.
It was a terrible forced march.
I found out about it later.
But yeah, we weren't-- we weren't expected to survive.
But we knew that the Russians were getting close.
I mean, these things-- it's amazing that even in Auschwitz, how rumors, and, in fact, facts could be circulated.
We knew, for instance, when the attempt was on Hitler's life.
We were-- I don't if it was in August, sometime in August, 1944.
We heard it.
We knew about it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How, I have no idea.
But these things-- these things were circulated.
These rumors and news were circulated.
But yes, we knew that the Russians are close.
We didn't know how close.
But then when the Germans, of course, left, and they just left us.
They just-- they were fleeing all over the place.
And then the next thing we knew, the first Russian soldiers showed up.
They looked at us, and they didn't know what to make of us.
I mean, obviously, they didn't expect to see what they saw.
I remember one Jewish officer was really, really upset.
Obviously in great pain to see what he saw.
But a lot of them weren't.
A lot of them were-- basically, they were primitive people, and they were fighting a war.
And the last thing they cared about is what happened to those people who are starving.
I mean, it's not that they were cruel to us in any way.
But they didn't have enough food to feed us.
I mean, they probably didn't have enough food for themselves.
So it wasn't like the American army.
When the Americans liberated places like Dachau and other camps, they had all the food and all the clothing, and the Russians didn't.
Well, they saw the-- the emaciated bodies of human beings.
And they saw the-- the wholesale starvation.
And of course, dead bodies, because there were always dead bodies around.
And so they saw what was happening, I mean, they saw the terrible conditions.
But like I said, it's-- they had a war to fight.
They weren't too interested.
In fact, as I recall-- and I don't know what happened later, because after they arrived, the next day we left.
So I don't know.
Maybe later on they were more organized.
I mean, these were the first troops, I suppose.
And I shouldn't pass judgment on them on the basis of what I saw, because it was just a very-- it was the front line.
But they didn't seem to care too much.
They were-- there was no organized assistance at that point.
Perhaps there was later.
Were they aggressive at all?
No, no, no, no no.
They were almost indifferent.
I mean, I didn't see too many of them.
But the ones who came, they were almost indifferent.
They were-- unless they were Jewish, they probably were indifferent.
So what happened at that point, there were still a lot of twins, but smaller.
As I mentioned to you, the older ones had left, most of them, on that forced march a couple of weeks before.
But the younger ones, like myself and my brother and some others-- say kids up to maybe age 14 or 15-- stayed behind.
And we were-- and also our leader, I should mention his name, because he was our leader during the-- basically Mengele made him into the person responsible for the twins.
He himself was a twin.
He had a twin sister who was somewhere else in the camp.
And his name was Mr.
Spiegel.
Spiegel, S-P-I-E-G-E-L.
He now calls himself Zvi Spiegel.
He's now-- he lives in Israel.
I hope he's still alive.
I haven't seen him lately.
I did meet him once or twice in Israel.
He-- he was a wonderful man.
He was a truly remarkable person.
And when-- during the time we were in Auschwitz and in Birkenau, and he took responsibility for us and for the kids, and made sure that what he could, he helped as much as he could.
He was also, of course, a prisoner.
I mean, he was-- he didn't have any powers, so to speak.
But after the liberation, the next day we decided, and the kids decided, we want to go.
And we asked him to help us.
He first kind of hesitated.
It was too much of a responsibility for him to take 30 or so kids with him.
I mean, he didn't know how things are.
I mean, the war was still on.
And how is he going to lead 30 or so kids into-- back into Hungary?
But he was persuaded, and we took off.
And we actually took off on foot and towards Krakow.
And you know, we-- we got lifts from-- from Russian soldiers in trucks.
We got on-- on trains occasionally.
We-- it took us some weeks.
We stayed, actually, in Krakow a few weeks.
And then we went on to-- towards Lwow, Lemberg.
But we could never get to there, because that was the Russian border at that point, so they wouldn't let us go.
We had to go back.
And then eventually, we arrived to Hungary.
I know we left-- as far as the dates, we left-- Auschwitz was liberated by the Russians on January 27th.
We left on the 28th, the next day.
And we were quite weak, but we somehow managed.
I recall an experiences like in Krakow, for instance, when we stayed there for three weeks.
We actually-- we had very little food, obviously.
And we were taught and-- and persuaded to actually go begging.
And we went door to door in Krakow to beg for food.
And I-- you know, and when you're-- when you're hungry and you have nothing to eat, and you're told, well, that's the way you can get food, you do it.
Yeah.
And sometimes the Russian gave us some-- Russians gave us some food.
Sometimes we had to beg.
And the local population, they didn't have too much food themselves, but-- but you know, they gave.
Some were nice, some were awful.
Like everywhere else, like any other group.
And-- but yes, eventually we left, on the 28th of January.
And we got to Hungary around March.
March-- actually, I didn't get back to Pecs until the 11th of March, I believe was the date.
So we had quite a few weeks of going all over Poland, Czechoslovakia, and into Hungary.
But back in March, we arrived back in Pecs, my brother and I.
We had no idea.

In those-- at that time, the general-- the conventional wisdom was that everybody should go back to their homes, because that's the only way you're going to find out if you can be reunited with your family.
I mean, there was-- the communications were nonexistent I suppose.
And the only way to find out if any of your other family members survived is to go back to your hometown and wait and see.
And when we got back-- of course, we were the first, actually, to get back to Pecs.
The first deportees that ever got back to Pecs was my brother and I.
And soon, you know, there's been a trickling of people coming back, and some months later, my father showed up.
He didn't know if we were alive.
We didn't know that he was alive until practically the time he arrived.
I think he called on the phone, just before he got to Pecs, and from some other town.
And that's when he found out that we were alive.
Yes, we did.
Eventually, we did.
I'm not sure.
I'm trying to remember how long it took, because there were people who lived there, I think.
But yes, eventually, we got our house back, of course.
I mean, that was-- at the time, of course, the Fascists were gone, and the Nazis were gone.
Excuse me.
And-- and we-- within a few months, I think, we were back in our house.
And we were waiting.
I mean, at that point, my father didn't know, and we didn't know what happened to my mother and sister.
We were still hoping, and I was hoping for a long time.
Well, it-- it was a relief, yes.
It was a relief.
And-- and he was in Dachau.
He was-- he was in one of those labor units in Hungary, and then he was deported to Dachau, in Dachau area.
And yes, it was quite a reunion.
It was obviously a-- a very happy reunion with my father.
And of course, it was, you know, the happiness was incomplete, because the rest of the family was still not there, and we-- we had no idea.
You could not really celebrate.
Well, after we got back, I was quite sick, actually, for a while.
We were both in pretty bad shape, both my brother and I.
I got scarlet fever and pneumonia.
Not pneumonia, pleurisy.
And so I was quite sick.
But eventually, we got back to school.
And we went back the same school, [INAUDIBLE], and stayed there, actually, for a number of years.
And my father got his business back.
He started to rebuild his business.
And I think he was fairly successful, nowhere near as successful as before the war.
But he would've been OK, until, of course, the communists took-- took it away from him again.
So at that point, what happened in Hungary, in 1945 until about 1947, there was a-- a semblance of democracy.
I think those were the two years of democracy in the 1,000 year history of Hungary.
[LAUGHS]
But it was a democratic system, and with multi-parties and elections, and all that.
In 1947, the communists, of course, the Russians were still there.
And they clamped down, and they took over the country by [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] doing some subterfuge.
And then by false elections and gimmicks, they took over the government.
And things got-- gone downhill ever since, or at least for a number of years after that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father's business was taken away.
And eventually we decided that-- or he decided-- well, yeah, I should mention before that that he remarried.
Back in-- I think it was 1947, yeah,-- he remarried, and my stepmother came to live  with us, of course, and she's a tremendous lady.
And she was very, very kind to us.
She's still alive, and we have a very good relationship.
But in 1947, when my father remarried, it was-- it was the first time when I truly realized that my mother would never come back.
And that was a shock, because until then, I was still hopeful.
And it took me that long to-- for it to sink in that I'll never see my mother and sister again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So what happened in '47, my father remarried.
We-- we lived in the same house as before the war.
And in 1949, he decided that basically, he wants to get out of the country.
And so he decided to send us out first, the two of us, my brother and I, to Israel.
And-- and that he and my stepmother will follow.
Can I take you back for just a moment?
Sure.
Well, a lot of people were scared.
You know, they were scared of what was going to happen.
What are the Jews going to do to us, you know?
What kind of revenge-- revenge are they going to meet us?
You know, with the-- will there be a real bloodbath, or-- or what?
Of course, nothing of that ever happened.
I mean, the Jews are not that kind of people.
And no, there were some who-- who were scared.
Some were genuinely relieved, I think.
And as far as the school is concerned, the teachers didn't pay too much attention.
You know, we got back.
At that point, they had no choice but take us back.
I don't think they would have dared not to.
But their-- their school was short lived.
I mean, the communist regime actually took over the school, and then the priests were all expelled.
And it became a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was nationalized, essentially.
So it became a public school from a private school.
No.
Very-- outside of the Jewish community?
No, very little.
I mean, no, I didn't.
Within the Jewish community, yes.
In fact, when we first arrived back home, we were bombarded with questions by people who either survived or-- or who stayed behind for whatever reason.
And we were constantly bombarded.
I mean, for weeks, we-- at some point, we were really just-- just exhausted, because people wanted to know, did I meet this person, or did I meet that person, did I see him or her.
And I mean, they were-- you know, they just wanted to know their own relatives.
And so there was a lot of-- it was really difficult for us.
At some point, it was quite trying to-- to answer all the questions.
We were-- we were interviewed left and right.
We were interviewed by the police, by the government, by all sorts of people.
I think about-- I'm guessing, but I think about 600-- 400 to 600-- survived.
Yeah.
I don't have the exact numbers, but, you know, it was in the hundreds.
Yeah.
OK.
